infobox.key: [Sergeant Major of the Army](/wiki/Sergeant_Major_of_the_Army "Sergeant Major of the Army")
infobox.value: [SMA](/wiki/Sergeant_Major_of_the_Army "Sergeant Major of the Army") [Michael Weimer](/wiki/Michael_Weimer "Michael Weimer")[[14]](#cite_note-14)

(**U.S. Army**) is the [land service](/wiki/Land_warfare "Land warfare") branch of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces"). It is designated as the [army](/wiki/Army "Army") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution").[[15]](#cite_note-Constitution-16) As a part of the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"), it is one of the six [armed forces of the United States](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") and one of the eight [uniformed services of the United States](/wiki/Uniformed_services_of_the_United_States "Uniformed services of the United States"). Founded in 1784, it succeeded the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army"), formed in 1775 during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECMH-12)

Military unit

The **United States Army** (**U.S. Army**) is the [land service](/wiki/Land_warfare "Land warfare") branch of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces"). It is designated as the [army](/wiki/Army "Army") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") in the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution").[[15]](#cite_note-Constitution-16) As a part of the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"), it is one of the six [armed forces of the United States](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") and one of the eight [uniformed services of the United States](/wiki/Uniformed_services_of_the_United_States "Uniformed services of the United States"). Founded in 1784, it succeeded the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army"), formed in 1775 during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECMH-12)

The U.S. Army is part of the [Department of the Army](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Army "United States Department of the Army"), which is one of the three military departments of the Department of Defense. The Army is headed by the civilian [secretary of the Army](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Army "United States Secretary of the Army"), and by the military [chief of staff of the Army](/wiki/Chief_of_Staff_of_the_United_States_Army "Chief of Staff of the United States Army"), a member of the [Joint Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Joint Chiefs of Staff"). It is the largest military branch, and for [FY](/wiki/Fiscal_year#United_States "Fiscal year")2022, the projected headcount of the Army was 1,005,725 soldiers: the [Regular Army](/wiki/Regular_Army_(United_States) "Regular Army (United States)") 480,893 soldiers; the [Army National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard") 336,129 soldiers, and the [U.S. Army Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Army_Reserve "United States Army Reserve") 188,703 soldiers.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Major branches include [Air Defense Artillery](/wiki/Air_Defense_Artillery_Branch "Air Defense Artillery Branch"), [Armor](/wiki/Armor_Branch "Armor Branch"), [Aviation](/wiki/United_States_Army_Aviation_Branch "United States Army Aviation Branch"), [Field Artillery](/wiki/Field_Artillery_Branch_(United_States) "Field Artillery Branch (United States)"), [Infantry](/wiki/Infantry_Branch_(United_States) "Infantry Branch (United States)"), and [Special Forces](/wiki/United_States_Army_Special_Forces "United States Army Special Forces"). With the world's highest vehicle-to-soldier ratio,[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18) the Army operates tens of thousands of combat vehicles, including the [M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank"), [Bradley](/wiki/Bradley_Fighting_Vehicle "Bradley Fighting Vehicle") [armoured fighting vehicle](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle"),[[18]](#cite_note-19) and [UH-60 Black Hawk](/wiki/Sikorsky_UH-60_Black_Hawk "Sikorsky UH-60 Black Hawk") helicopters.[[19]](#cite_note-20) The Army also deploys key systems for the [Missile Defense Agency](/wiki/Missile_Defense_Agency "Missile Defense Agency"): the [Ground-Based Midcourse Defense](/wiki/Ground-Based_Midcourse_Defense "Ground-Based Midcourse Defense"),[[20]](#cite_note-21) [Terminal High Altitude Area Defense](/wiki/Terminal_High_Altitude_Area_Defense "Terminal High Altitude Area Defense"),[[21]](#cite_note-22) and [MIM-104 Patriot](/wiki/MIM-104_Patriot "MIM-104 Patriot").[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The U.S. Army fought the [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars") (1784–1890), the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") (1812–15), [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") (1846–48). The [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") (1861–65) was its costliest conflict, with over 350,000 casualties.[[23]](#cite_note-24) It fought the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") (1898), [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") (1917–18), [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") (1941–45). In the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the Army fought the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") (1950–53) and [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") (1965–71), and operated [tactical nuclear weapons](/wiki/Tactical_nuclear_weapon "Tactical nuclear weapon") ranging from the [Pershing II](/wiki/Pershing_II "Pershing II") [ballistic missile](/wiki/Ballistic_missile "Ballistic missile") to the [Davy Crockett](/wiki/Davy_Crockett_(nuclear_device) "Davy Crockett (nuclear device)") [rifle](/wiki/Recoilless_rifle "Recoilless rifle").[[24]](#cite_note-25) Following the [Cold War's end](/wiki/Cold_War_(1985%E2%80%931991) "Cold War (1985–1991)") in 1991, Army has focused primarily on [Western Asia](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia") and [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency") roles, including the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)"), and [war in Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"). The U.S. Army currently provides materiel and training [to Ukraine](/wiki/United_States_and_the_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine#Military_assistance_to_Ukraine "United States and the Russian invasion of Ukraine") in the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

## Mission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mission")]

The United States Army serves as the primary land-based branch of the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense"). [Section 7062 of Title 10, U.S. Code](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/10/7062) defines the purpose of the army as:[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

* Preserving the peace and security and providing for the defense of the United States, the Commonwealths and possessions, and any areas occupied by the United States
* Supporting the national policies
* Implementing the national objectives
* Overcoming any nations responsible for aggressive acts that imperil the peace and security of the United States

In 2018, the *Army Strategy 2018* articulated an eight-point addendum to the Army Vision for 2028.[[29]](#cite_note-Strategy2018-30) While the Army Mission remains constant, the Army Strategy builds upon the Army's [Brigade Modernization](/wiki/Brigade_Modernization_Command "Brigade Modernization Command") by adding focus to [corps](/wiki/Corps "Corps") and [division](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)")-level echelons.[[29]](#cite_note-Strategy2018-30) The [Army Futures Command](/wiki/Army_Futures_Command "Army Futures Command") oversees reforms geared toward [conventional warfare](/wiki/Conventional_warfare "Conventional warfare"). The Army's current [reorganization plan](/wiki/Reorganization_plan_of_United_States_Army "Reorganization plan of United States Army") is due to be completed by 2028.[[29]](#cite_note-Strategy2018-30)

The Army's five core competencies are prompt and sustained land combat, [combined arms](/wiki/Combined_arms "Combined arms") operations (to include combined arms maneuver and wide–area security, [armored](/wiki/Armored_forces "Armored forces") and [mechanized operations](/wiki/Mechanized_infantry "Mechanized infantry") and [airborne](/wiki/Airborne_forces "Airborne forces") and [air assault operations](/wiki/Air_assault_infantry "Air assault infantry")), [special operations forces](/wiki/Special_operations_forces "Special operations forces"), to set and sustain the theater for the joint force and to integrate national, multinational, and joint power on land.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the United States Army](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Army "History of the United States Army")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

The Continental Army was created on 14 June 1775 by the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress")[[31]](#cite_note-32) as a unified army for the colonies to fight [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), with [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") appointed as its commander.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECMH-12)[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) The army was initially led by men who had served in the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") or colonial militias and who brought much of British military heritage with them. As the Revolutionary War progressed, [French](/wiki/Early_Modern_France "Early Modern France") aid, resources, and military thinking helped shape the new army. A number of European soldiers came on their own to help, such as [Friedrich Wilhelm von Steuben](/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_von_Steuben "Friedrich Wilhelm von Steuben"), who taught [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") tactics and organizational skills.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Bataille_Yorktown.jpg/250px-Bataille_Yorktown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bataille_Yorktown.jpg)

The storming of Redoubt No. 10 in the [Siege of Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), as depicted in a [watercolor painting](/wiki/Watercolor_painting "Watercolor painting") by [H. Charles McBarron Jr.](/wiki/H._Charles_McBarron_Jr. "H. Charles McBarron Jr.") (1902–1992) prompted Great Britain's government to begin negotiations, resulting in the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") and Great Britain's recognition of the United States as an independent state.

The Army fought numerous pitched battles, and sometimes used [Fabian strategy](/wiki/Fabian_strategy "Fabian strategy") and [hit-and-run tactics](/wiki/Hit-and-run_tactics "Hit-and-run tactics") in the South in 1780 and 1781; under Major General [Nathanael Greene](/wiki/Nathanael_Greene "Nathanael Greene"), it hit where the British were weakest to wear down their forces. Washington led victories against the British at [Trenton](/wiki/Battle_of_Trenton "Battle of Trenton") and [Princeton](/wiki/Battle_of_Princeton "Battle of Princeton"), but lost a series of battles in the [New York and New Jersey campaign](/wiki/New_York_and_New_Jersey_campaign "New York and New Jersey campaign") in 1776 and the [Philadelphia campaign](/wiki/Philadelphia_campaign "Philadelphia campaign") in 1777. With a decisive victory at [Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") and the help of the French, the Continental Army prevailed against the British.

After the war, the Continental Army was quickly given land certificates and disbanded in a reflection of the [republican](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_States "Republicanism in the United States") distrust of standing armies. [State](/wiki/US_state "US state") militias became the new nation's sole ground army, except [a regiment](/wiki/First_American_Regiment "First American Regiment") to guard the [Western Frontier](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") and one battery of [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") guarding [West Point](/wiki/West_Point "West Point")'s arsenal. However, because of continuing conflict with [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), it was soon considered necessary to field a trained standing army. The [Regular Army](/wiki/Regular_Army_(United_States) "Regular Army (United States)") was at first very small and after General [St. Clair's defeat](/wiki/St._Clair%27s_defeat "St. Clair's defeat") at the Battle of the Wabash,[[35]](#cite_note-Buffenbarger-36) where more than 800 soldiers were killed, the Regular Army was reorganized as the [Legion of the United States](/wiki/Legion_of_the_United_States "Legion of the United States"), established in 1791 and renamed the United States Army in 1796.

In 1798, during the [Quasi-War](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War") with France, the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") established a three-year "[Provisional Army](/wiki/Provisional_Army_of_the_United_States "Provisional Army of the United States")" of 10,000 men, consisting of twelve [regiments](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") of [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") and six troops of light [dragoons](/wiki/Dragoon "Dragoon"). In March 1799, Congress created an "Eventual Army" of 30,000 men, including three regiments of [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry"). Both "armies" existed only on paper, but equipment for 3,000 men and horses was procured and stored.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

Further information: [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") and [Army on the Frontier](/wiki/Army_on_the_Frontier "Army on the Frontier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Battle_of_New_Orleans.jpg/250px-Battle_of_New_Orleans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_New_Orleans.jpg)

General [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") standing on the parapet of his makeshift defenses as his troops repulse attacking [Highlanders](/wiki/93rd_(Sutherland_Highlanders)_Regiment_of_Foot "93rd (Sutherland Highlanders) Regiment of Foot") during the [defense of New Orleans](/wiki/Battle_of_New_Orleans "Battle of New Orleans"), the final major and most one-sided battle of the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), mainly fought by militia and volunteers.

#### War of 1812

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: War of 1812")]

Main article: [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812")

[The War of 1812](/wiki/The_War_of_1812 "The War of 1812") was the second and last war between the United States and Great Britain. The war was split between a Northern, Southern, and naval campaign.[[37]](#cite_note-38) While a large part of the war was fought between the United States and Great Britain, there were a variety of native tribes that fought on both sides of the conflict. The result of the war is the [Treaty of Ghent](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ghent "Treaty of Ghent") and is generally considered to be inconclusive, and brought upon a period of peace between the United States and Great Britain that has lasted for over two centuries.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

#### Seminole Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Seminole Wars")]

Main article: [Seminole Wars](/wiki/Seminole_Wars "Seminole Wars")

There was a long period of war between the United States and the [Seminoles](/wiki/Seminoles "Seminoles") that lasted over 50 years. The usual strategies utilized against Native American tribes were to seize winter food supplies and to form alliances with enemies of a tribe. These were not viable options against the Seminoles, largely due to the fact of lack of climate variability in Florida and because of the long history of warring between the Seminole tribe and other tribes in the Florida region.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

#### Mexican–American War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mexican–American War")]

Main article: [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War")

The U.S. Army fought and won the Mexican–American War, which was a defining event for both countries. The U.S. victory resulted in the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo"), in which Mexico [ceded](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession") a large portion of land to the United States which included the modern-day states of [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), and parts of [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") and [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming").[[40]](#cite_note-41)

#### American Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: American Civil War")]

Further information: [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Thure_de_Thulstrup_-_L._Prang_and_Co._-_Battle_of_Gettysburg_-_Restoration_by_Adam_Cuerden.jpg/250px-Thure_de_Thulstrup_-_L._Prang_and_Co._-_Battle_of_Gettysburg_-_Restoration_by_Adam_Cuerden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thure_de_Thulstrup_-_L._Prang_and_Co._-_Battle_of_Gettysburg_-_Restoration_by_Adam_Cuerden.jpg)

The [Battle of Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg"), the turning point of the American Civil War

The [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") was the costliest war for the U.S. in terms of casualties. After most [slave states](/wiki/Slave_state "Slave state"), located in the southern U.S., formed the [Confederate States](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), the [Confederate States Army](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army"), led by former U.S. Army officers, mobilized a large fraction of Southern white manpower. Forces of the United States (the "Union" or "the North") formed the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army"), consisting of a small body of regular army units and a large body of volunteer units raised from every state, north and south, except [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

For the first two years, Confederate forces did well in set battles but lost control of the border states.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The Confederates had the advantage of defending a large territory in an area where disease caused twice as many deaths as combat. The Union pursued a strategy of seizing the coastline, blockading the ports, and taking control of the river systems. By 1863, the Confederacy was being strangled. Its eastern armies fought well, but the western armies were defeated one after another until the Union forces captured New Orleans in 1862 along with the Tennessee River. In the [Vicksburg Campaign](/wiki/Vicksburg_Campaign "Vicksburg Campaign") of 1862–1863, General [Ulysses Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_Grant "Ulysses Grant") seized the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and cut off the Southwest. Grant took command of Union forces in 1864 and after a series of battles with very heavy casualties, he had General [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee") under siege in Richmond as General [William T. Sherman](/wiki/William_T._Sherman "William T. Sherman") captured Atlanta and [marched through Georgia](/wiki/Sherman%27s_March_to_the_Sea "Sherman's March to the Sea") and [the Carolinas](/wiki/The_Carolinas "The Carolinas"). The Confederate capital was abandoned in April 1865 and Lee subsequently surrendered his army at Appomattox Court House. All other Confederate armies surrendered within a few months.

The war remains the deadliest conflict in U.S. history, resulting in the deaths of 620,000 men on both sides. Based on 1860 census figures, 8% of all white males aged 13 to 43 died in the war, including 6.4% in [the North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") and 18% in [the South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").[[43]](#cite_note-44)

#### Later 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Later 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Soldiers_of_1890.jpg/250px-Soldiers_of_1890.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldiers_of_1890.jpg)

Army soldiers in 1890

Following the Civil War, the U.S. Army had the mission of containing western tribes of Native Americans on the [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation"). They set up many forts, and engaged in the last of the [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars"). U.S. Army troops also occupied several Southern states during the [Reconstruction Era](/wiki/Reconstruction_Era "Reconstruction Era") to protect [freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen").

The key battles of the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War") of 1898 were fought by the Navy. Using mostly [new volunteers](/wiki/United_States_Volunteers "United States Volunteers"), the U.S. forces defeated [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") in land campaigns in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") and played the central role in the [Philippine–American War](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War").

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Starting in 1910, the army began acquiring [fixed-wing aircraft](/wiki/Fixed-wing_aircraft "Fixed-wing aircraft").[[44]](#cite_note-45) In 1910, during the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution"), the army was deployed to U.S. towns near the border to ensure the safety of lives and property. In 1916, [Pancho Villa](/wiki/Pancho_Villa "Pancho Villa"), a major rebel leader, attacked [Columbus, New Mexico](/wiki/Columbus,_New_Mexico "Columbus, New Mexico"), prompting a [U.S. intervention in Mexico](/wiki/Pancho_Villa_Expedition "Pancho Villa Expedition") until 7 February 1917. They fought the rebels and the Mexican federal troops until 1918.

#### World Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: World Wars")]

Main article: [United States Army during World War II](/wiki/United_States_Army_during_World_War_II "United States Army during World War II")

For a list of campaigns see [List of United States Army campaigns during World War II](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Army_campaigns_during_World_War_II "List of United States Army campaigns during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/At_close_grips2.jpg/250px-At_close_grips2.jpg)](/wiki/File:At_close_grips2.jpg)

U.S. Army troops assaulting a German bunker in [France](/wiki/France "France"), c. 1918

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Us_army_world_war_1_johnstown_pennsylvania_common_history.jpg/250px-Us_army_world_war_1_johnstown_pennsylvania_common_history.jpg)](/wiki/File:Us_army_world_war_1_johnstown_pennsylvania_common_history.jpg)

U.S. Army unit before heading to France during World War I

The [United States joined World War I](/wiki/American_entry_into_World_War_I "American entry into World War I") as an "Associated Power" in 1917 on the side of [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic"), [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") and the other [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"). U.S. troops were sent to the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") and were involved in the last offensives that ended the war. With the armistice in November 1918, the army once again decreased its forces.

In 1939, estimates of the Army's strength ranged between 174,000 and 200,000 soldiers, smaller than that of [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal")'s, which ranked it 17th or 19th in the world in size. General [George C. Marshall](/wiki/George_C._Marshall "George C. Marshall") became Army chief of staff in September 1939 and set about expanding and modernizing the Army in preparation for war.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/U.S._Soldiers_at_Bougainville_%28Solomon_Islands%29_March_1944.jpg/250px-U.S._Soldiers_at_Bougainville_%28Solomon_Islands%29_March_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Soldiers_at_Bougainville_(Solomon_Islands)_March_1944.jpg)

U.S. soldiers hunting for Japanese infiltrators during the [Bougainville Campaign](/wiki/Bougainville_Campaign "Bougainville Campaign")

The United States joined [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in December 1941 after the [Japanese](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor"). Some 11 million Americans were to serve in various Army operations.[[47]](#cite_note-debruyne-48)[[48]](#cite_note-amh-v2-49) On the [European front](/wiki/European_Theatre_of_World_War_II "European Theatre of World War II"), U.S. Army troops formed a significant portion of the forces that landed in French North Africa and [took Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia_campaign "Tunisia campaign") and then [moved on to Sicily](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Sicily "Allied invasion of Sicily") and later [fought in Italy](/wiki/Italian_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian Campaign (World War II)"). In the June 1944 [landings in northern France](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") and in the subsequent [liberation of Europe](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_II)#1944–45:_The_Second_Front "Western Front (World War II)") and defeat of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), millions of U.S. Army troops played a central role. In 1947, the number of soldiers in the US Army had decreased from eight million in 1945 to 684,000 soldiers and the total number of active divisions had dropped from 89 to 12. The leaders of the Army saw this demobilization as a success.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

In the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War"), U.S. Army Soldiers made up the vast majority of ground forces there, capturing the [Pacific Islands](/wiki/Pacific_Islands "Pacific Islands") from Japanese control. In total, there were 86 amphibious landings throughout the Pacific. The Army conducted 71. Following the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers") surrenders in May (Germany) and August (Japan) of 1945, Army troops were deployed to Japan and Germany to occupy the two defeated nations. Two years after World War II, the [Army Air Forces](/wiki/Army_Air_Forces "Army Air Forces") separated from the Army to become the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") in September 1947. In 1948, the Army was [desegregated](/wiki/Desegregation_in_the_United_States "Desegregation in the United States") by [order 9981](/wiki/Executive_Order_9981 "Executive Order 9981") of President [Harry S. Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman").

#### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cold War")]

##### 1945–1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1945–1960")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Exercise_Desert_Rock_I_%28Buster-Jangle_Dog%29_003.jpg/250px-Exercise_Desert_Rock_I_%28Buster-Jangle_Dog%29_003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exercise_Desert_Rock_I_(Buster-Jangle_Dog)_003.jpg)

U.S. Army soldiers observing an atomic bomb test of [Operation Buster-Jangle](/wiki/Operation_Buster-Jangle "Operation Buster-Jangle") at the [Nevada Test Site](/wiki/Nevada_Test_Site "Nevada Test Site") during the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War")

The end of World War II set the stage for the East–West confrontation known as the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). With the outbreak of the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), concerns over the defense of Western Europe rose. Two corps, [V](/wiki/V_Corps_(United_States) "V Corps (United States)") and [VII](/wiki/VII_Corps_(United_States) "VII Corps (United States)"), were reactivated under [Seventh United States Army](/wiki/Seventh_United_States_Army "Seventh United States Army") in 1950 and U.S. strength in Europe rose from one division to four. Hundreds of thousands of U.S. troops remained stationed in West Germany, with others in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), until the 1990s in anticipation of a possible [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") attack.[[50]](#cite_note-PerkinsAOC-51): minute 9:00–10:00

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/US_Army_tanks_face_off_against_Soviet_tanks%2C_Berlin_1961.jpg/250px-US_Army_tanks_face_off_against_Soviet_tanks%2C_Berlin_1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Army_tanks_face_off_against_Soviet_tanks,_Berlin_1961.jpg)

US tanks and Soviet tanks at [Checkpoint Charlie](/wiki/Checkpoint_Charlie "Checkpoint Charlie"), 1961

During the Cold War, U.S. troops and their allies fought [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") forces in Korea and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"). The Korean War began in June 1950, when the Soviets walked out of a UN Security Council meeting, removing their possible veto. Under a [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") umbrella, hundreds of thousands of U.S. troops fought to prevent the takeover of [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") by [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") and later to invade the northern nation. After repeated advances and retreats by both sides and the Chinese [People's Volunteer Army](/wiki/People%27s_Volunteer_Army "People's Volunteer Army")'s entry into the war, the [Korean Armistice Agreement](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Korean_Armistice_Agreement "s:Korean Armistice Agreement") returned the peninsula to the status quo in July 1953.

##### 1960–1970

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1960–1970")]

Main article: [United States Army during Vietnam War](/wiki/United_States_Army_during_Vietnam_War "United States Army during Vietnam War")

The [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") is often regarded as a low point for the U.S. Army due to the use of [drafted personnel](/wiki/The_Draft "The Draft"), the unpopularity of the war with the U.S. public and frustrating restrictions placed on the military by U.S. political leaders. While U.S. forces had been stationed in [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam") since 1959, in intelligence and advising/training roles, they were not deployed in large numbers until 1965, after the [Gulf of Tonkin Incident](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tonkin_Incident "Gulf of Tonkin Incident"). U.S. forces effectively established and maintained control of the "traditional" battlefield, but they struggled to counter the [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla_war "Guerrilla war") hit and run tactics of the communist [Viet Cong](/wiki/National_Front_for_the_Liberation_of_South_Vietnam "National Front for the Liberation of South Vietnam") and the [People's Army of Vietnam (NVA)](/wiki/People%27s_Army_of_Vietnam "People's Army of Vietnam").[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/DakToVietnam1966.jpg/250px-DakToVietnam1966.jpg)](/wiki/File:DakToVietnam1966.jpg)

A U.S. Army infantry patrol moving up to assault the last [North Vietnamese Army](/wiki/North_Vietnamese_Army "North Vietnamese Army") position at Dak To, South Vietnam during [Operation Hawthorne](/wiki/Operation_Hawthorne "Operation Hawthorne")

During the 1960s, the Department of Defense continued to scrutinize the reserve forces and to question the number of divisions and brigades as well as the redundancy of maintaining two reserve components, the [Army National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard") and the [Army Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Army_Reserve "United States Army Reserve").[[53]](#cite_note-54) In 1967, Secretary of Defense [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara") decided that 15 combat divisions in the Army National Guard were unnecessary and cut the number to eight divisions (one mechanized infantry, two armored, and five infantry), but increased the number of brigades from seven to 18 (one airborne, one armored, two mechanized infantry and 14 infantry). The loss of the divisions did not sit well with the states. Their objections included the inadequate maneuver element mix for those that remained and the end to the practice of rotating divisional commands among the states that supported them. Under the proposal, the remaining division commanders were to reside in the state of the division base. However, no reduction in total Army National Guard strength was to take place, which convinced the governors to accept the plan. The states reorganized their forces accordingly between 1 December 1967 and 1 May 1968.

##### 1970–1990

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1970–1990")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Operation_Just_Cause_Rangers_3rd_sqd_la_comadancia_small.jpg/250px-Operation_Just_Cause_Rangers_3rd_sqd_la_comadancia_small.jpg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Just_Cause_Rangers_3rd_sqd_la_comadancia_small.jpg)

U.S. Army soldiers preparing to take *La Comandancia* in the El Chorrillo neighborhood of [Panama City](/wiki/Panama_City "Panama City") during [Operation Just Cause](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Panama "United States invasion of Panama")

The Total Force Policy was adopted by Chief of Staff of the Army General [Creighton Abrams](/wiki/Creighton_Abrams "Creighton Abrams") in the aftermath of the Vietnam War and involved treating the three components of the army – the [Regular Army](/wiki/Regular_Army_(United_States) "Regular Army (United States)"), the [Army National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard") and the [Army Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Army_Reserve "United States Army Reserve") as a single force.[[54]](#cite_note-Army_National_Guard_Constitution-55) General Abrams' intertwining of the three components of the army effectively made extended operations impossible without the involvement of both the Army National Guard and Army Reserve in a predominantly combat support role.[[55]](#cite_note-56) The army converted to an all-volunteer force with greater emphasis on training to specific performance standards driven by the reforms of General [William E. DePuy](/wiki/William_E._DePuy "William E. DePuy"), the first commander of [United States Army Training and Doctrine Command](/wiki/United_States_Army_Training_and_Doctrine_Command "United States Army Training and Doctrine Command"). Following the Camp David Accords that was signed by Egypt, Israel that was brokered by president [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") in 1978, as part of the agreement, both the United States and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") agreed that there would be a joint military training led by both countries that would usually take place every 2 years, that exercise is known as [Exercise Bright Star](/wiki/Exercise_Bright_Star "Exercise Bright Star").

The 1980s was mostly a decade of reorganization. The [Goldwater–Nichols Act](/wiki/Goldwater%E2%80%93Nichols_Act "Goldwater–Nichols Act") of 1986 created [unified combatant commands](/wiki/Unified_Combatant_Command "Unified Combatant Command") bringing the army together with the other four [military services](/wiki/United_States_Military "United States Military") under unified, geographically organized command structures. The army also played a role in the invasions of [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada") in 1983 ([Operation Urgent Fury](/wiki/Invasion_of_Grenada "Invasion of Grenada")) and [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama") in 1989 ([Operation Just Cause](/wiki/Operation_Just_Cause "Operation Just Cause")).

By 1989, [Germany was nearing reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") and the Cold War was coming to a close. Army leadership reacted by starting to plan for a reduction in strength. By November 1989 Pentagon briefers were laying out plans to reduce army end strength by 23%, from 750,000 to 580,000.[[56]](#cite_note-57) A number of incentives such as early retirement were used.

#### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1990s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Abrams_in_formation.jpg/250px-Abrams_in_formation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abrams_in_formation.jpg)

[M1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") tanks moving out before the [Battle of Al Busayyah](/wiki/Battle_of_Al_Busayyah "Battle of Al Busayyah") during the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War")

In 1990, [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") [invaded](/wiki/Invasion_of_Kuwait "Invasion of Kuwait") its smaller neighbor, [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), and U.S. land forces quickly deployed to assure the protection of [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"). In January 1991 [Operation Desert Storm](/wiki/Operation_Desert_Storm "Operation Desert Storm") commenced, a U.S.-led coalition which deployed over 500,000 troops, the bulk of them from U.S. Army formations, to [drive out Iraqi forces](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"). The campaign ended in total victory, as Western coalition forces routed the [Iraqi Army](/wiki/Iraqi_Army "Iraqi Army"). Some of the largest tank battles in history were fought during the Gulf war. The [Battle of Medina Ridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Medina_Ridge "Battle of Medina Ridge"), [Battle of Norfolk](/wiki/Battle_of_Norfolk "Battle of Norfolk") and the [Battle of 73 Easting](/wiki/Battle_of_73_Easting "Battle of 73 Easting") were tank battles of historical significance.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Destroyed_Iraqi_tank_TF-41.jpg/250px-Destroyed_Iraqi_tank_TF-41.jpg)](/wiki/File:Destroyed_Iraqi_tank_TF-41.jpg)

Iraqi tanks destroyed by [Task Force 1-41 Infantry](/wiki/Task_Force_1-41_Infantry "Task Force 1-41 Infantry") during the Gulf War, February 1991

After Operation Desert Storm, the army did not see major combat operations for the remainder of the 1990s but did participate in a number of peacekeeping activities. In 1990 the Department of Defense issued guidance for "rebalancing" after a review of the Total Force Policy,[[60]](#cite_note-61) but in 2004, [USAF Air War College](/wiki/USAF_Air_War_College "USAF Air War College") scholars concluded the guidance would reverse the Total Force Policy which is an "essential ingredient to the successful application of military force".[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Flickr_-_DVIDSHUB_-_Operation_in_Nahr-e_Saraj_%28Image_5_of_7%29.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_DVIDSHUB_-_Operation_in_Nahr-e_Saraj_%28Image_5_of_7%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_DVIDSHUB_-_Operation_in_Nahr-e_Saraj_(Image_5_of_7).jpg)

U.S. Army Rangers taking part in a raid during an operation in [Nahr-e Saraj](/wiki/Nahr-e_Saraj_District "Nahr-e Saraj District"), Afghanistan

On 11 September 2001, 53 Army civilians (47 employees and six contractors) and 22 soldiers were among the 125 victims killed in [the Pentagon](/wiki/Casualties_of_the_September_11_attacks#Pentagon "Casualties of the September 11 attacks") in a [terrorist attack](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") when [American Airlines Flight 77](/wiki/American_Airlines_Flight_77 "American Airlines Flight 77") commandeered by five [Al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") [hijackers](/wiki/Aircraft_hijacking "Aircraft hijacking") slammed into the western side of the building, as part of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[62]](#cite_note-63) In response to the 11 September attacks and as part of the global [war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror"), U.S. and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") forces [invaded Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") in October 2001, displacing the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban") government. The US Army were the first into Afghanistan, with the 10th Mountain Division being the first conventional unit to arrive in country in 2001.[[63]](#cite_note-64) The U.S. Army also led the combined U.S. and allied [invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq") in 2003; it served as the primary source for ground forces with its ability to sustain short and long-term deployment operations. Like Afghanistan, the US Army was the first into Iraq.[[64]](#cite_note-65) In the following years, the mission changed from conflict between regular militaries to [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency"), resulting in the deaths of more than 4,000 U.S. service members (as of March 2008) and injuries to thousands more.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-Lancet_supplement-67) 23,813 insurgents were killed in Iraq between 2003 and 2011.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/U.S._Soldiers_with_the_1st_Battalion%2C_32nd_Infantry_Regiment%2C_3rd_Brigade_Combat_Team%2C_10th_Mountain_Division_take_cover_during_an_ambush_by_enemy_forces_in_Lachey_village%2C_Shigal_district%2C_Kunar_province_091207-A-AO884-330.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Soldiers_with_the_1st_Battalion,_32nd_Infantry_Regiment,_3rd_Brigade_Combat_Team,_10th_Mountain_Division_take_cover_during_an_ambush_by_enemy_forces_in_Lachey_village,_Shigal_district,_Kunar_province_091207-A-AO884-330.jpg)

U.S. Army Soldiers with the [10th Mountain Division](/wiki/10th_Mountain_Division "10th Mountain Division") take cover during an ambush by enemy forces in Kunar province, Afghanistan, 2009

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/U.S._Army_firefight_in_Kunar.jpg/250px-U.S._Army_firefight_in_Kunar.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Army_firefight_in_Kunar.jpg)

U.S. Army soldiers with the 2nd Battalion, [327th Infantry Regiment](/wiki/327th_Infantry_Regiment "327th Infantry Regiment"), [101st Airborne Division](/wiki/101st_Airborne_Division "101st Airborne Division") returning fire during a firefight with Taliban forces in Barawala Kalay Valley in [Kunar province](/wiki/Kunar_Province "Kunar Province"), Afghanistan, March 2011

Until 2009, the Army's chief modernization plan, its most ambitious since World War II,[[68]](#cite_note-69) was the [Future Combat Systems](/wiki/Future_Combat_Systems "Future Combat Systems") program. In 2009, many systems were canceled, and the remaining were swept into the [BCT modernization program](/wiki/BCT_Modernization "BCT Modernization").[[69]](#cite_note-70) By 2017, the Brigade Modernization project was completed and its headquarters, the Brigade Modernization Command, was renamed the Joint Modernization Command, or JMC.[[70]](#cite_note-71) In response to [Budget sequestration in 2013](/wiki/Budget_sequestration_in_2013 "Budget sequestration in 2013"), Army plans were to shrink to 1940 levels,[[71]](#cite_note-72) although actual Active-Army end-strengths were projected to fall to some 450,000 troops by the end of FY2017.[[72]](#cite_note-troopLevels2015Plan-73)[[73]](#cite_note-authorizedStrength2020-74) From 2016 to 2017, the Army retired hundreds of [OH-58 Kiowa Warrior](/wiki/OH-58_Kiowa_Warrior "OH-58 Kiowa Warrior") observation helicopters,[[74]](#cite_note-retired-75) while retaining its Apache gunships.[[75]](#cite_note-faraScoutCapability-76) The 2015 expenditure for Army research, development and acquisition changed from $32 billion projected in 2012 for FY15 to $21 billion for FY15 expected in 2014.[[76]](#cite_note-Drw-77)

The US Army saw extensive combat throughout both Afghanistan and Iraq. The [10th Mountain Division](/wiki/10th_Mountain_Division "10th Mountain Division") was the most deployed unit in the US military during the [Global War on Terror](/wiki/Global_War_on_Terror "Global War on Terror").[[77]](#cite_note-78)

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Organization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Organization_of_U.S._Space_Force.svg/500px-Organization_of_U.S._Space_Force.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Organization_of_U.S._Space_Force.svg)

Organization of the United States Army within the Department of Defense

### Planning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Planning")]

By 2017, a task force was formed to address Army modernization,[[78]](#cite_note-afcTF2017-79) which triggered shifts of units: [CCDC](/wiki/United_States_Army_Combat_Capabilities_Development_Command "United States Army Combat Capabilities Development Command"), and [ARCIC](/wiki/United_States_Army_Capabilities_Integration_Center "United States Army Capabilities Integration Center"), from within [Army Materiel Command](/wiki/United_States_Army_Materiel_Command "United States Army Materiel Command") (AMC), and [Army Training and Doctrine Command](/wiki/United_States_Army_Training_and_Doctrine_Command "United States Army Training and Doctrine Command") (TRADOC), respectively, to a new Army Command (ACOM) in 2018.[[79]](#cite_note-G.O.2018-10-80) AFC's mission is modernization reform: to design hardware, as well as to work within the acquisition process which defines materiel for AMC. TRADOC's mission is to define the architecture and organization of the Army, and to train and supply soldiers to FORSCOM.[[80]](#cite_note-81): minutes 2:30–15:00[[50]](#cite_note-PerkinsAOC-51) [AFC's cross-functional teams (CFTs)](/wiki/United_States_Army_Futures_Command#Cross-functional_teams "United States Army Futures Command") are Futures Command's vehicle for sustainable [reform of the acquisition process](/wiki/United_States_Army_Futures_Command#Need_for_modernization_reform "United States Army Futures Command") for the future.[[81]](#cite_note-acqReformCulture-82) In order to support the Army's modernization priorities, its FY2020 budget allocated $30 billion for the top six modernization priorities over the next five years.[[82]](#cite_note-fy2020,30billion-83) The $30 billion came from $8 billion in cost avoidance and $22 billion in terminations.[[82]](#cite_note-fy2020,30billion-83)

### Army Components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Army Components")]

See also: [Structure of the United States Army](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Army "Structure of the United States Army")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/DA_Pam_10-1_Figure_1-1.png/330px-DA_Pam_10-1_Figure_1-1.png)](/wiki/File:DA_Pam_10-1_Figure_1-1.png)

U.S. Army organization chart[[83]](#cite_note-84)

The task of organizing the U.S. Army commenced in 1775.[[84]](#cite_note-85) In the first one hundred years of its existence, the United States Army was maintained as a small peacetime force to man permanent [forts](/wiki/Forts "Forts") and perform other non-wartime duties such as [engineering](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Engineers") and construction works. During times of war, the U.S. Army was augmented by the much larger [United States Volunteers](/wiki/United_States_Volunteers "United States Volunteers") which were raised independently by various state governments. States also maintained full-time [militias](/wiki/Militia "Militia") which could also be called into the service of the army.

By the twentieth century, the U.S. Army had mobilized the U.S. Volunteers on four occasions during each of the major wars of the nineteenth century. During World War I, the "[National Army](/wiki/National_Army_(USA) "National Army (USA)")" was organized to fight the conflict, replacing the concept of U.S. Volunteers.[[85]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-86) It was demobilized at the end of World War I and was replaced by the Regular Army, the Organized Reserve Corps, and the state militias. In the 1920s and 1930s, the "career" soldiers were known as the "[Regular Army](/wiki/Regular_Army_(United_States) "Regular Army (United States)")" with the "Enlisted Reserve Corps" and "Officer Reserve Corps" augmented to fill vacancies when needed.[[86]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-87)

In 1941, the "[Army of the United States](/wiki/Army_of_the_United_States "Army of the United States")" was founded to fight World War II.[[87]](#cite_note-88) The Regular Army, Army of the United States, the National Guard, and Officer/Enlisted Reserve Corps (ORC and ERC) existed simultaneously. After World War II, the ORC and ERC were combined into the [United States Army Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Army_Reserve "United States Army Reserve"). The Army of the United States was re-established for the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") and was demobilized upon the suspension of the [draft](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_States "Conscription in the United States").[[86]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-87)

Currently, the Army is divided into the [Regular Army](/wiki/Regular_Army_(United_States) "Regular Army (United States)"), the [Army Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Army_Reserve "United States Army Reserve"), and the [Army National Guard](/wiki/Army_National_Guard "Army National Guard").[[85]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-86) Some states further maintain [state defense forces](/wiki/State_defense_force "State defense force"), as a type of reserve to the National Guard, while all states maintain regulations for [state militias](/wiki/Militia_(United_States) "Militia (United States)").[[88]](#cite_note-89)

The U.S. Army is also divided into [several branches and functional areas](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Army_careers "List of United States Army careers"). Branches include officers, warrant officers, and enlisted Soldiers while functional areas consist of officers who are reclassified from their former branch into a functional area. However, officers continue to wear the [branch insignia](/wiki/United_States_Army_branch_insignia "United States Army branch insignia") of their former branch in most cases, as functional areas do not generally have discrete insignia. Some branches, such as [Special Forces](/wiki/United_States_Army_Special_Forces "United States Army Special Forces"), operate similarly to functional areas in that individuals may not join their ranks until having served in another Army branch. Careers in the Army can extend into cross-functional areas for officers,[[89]](#cite_note-AACSL-90) warrant officers, enlisted, and civilian personnel.

Before 1933, the Army National Guard members were considered state militia until they were mobilized into the U.S. Army, typically at the onset of war. Since the 1933 amendment to the [National Defense Act of 1916](/wiki/National_Defense_Act_of_1916 "National Defense Act of 1916"), all Army National Guard soldiers have held dual status. They serve as National Guardsmen under the authority of the governor of their state or territory and as reserve members of the U.S. Army under the authority of the president, in the Army National Guard of the United States.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

Since the adoption of the total force policy, in the aftermath of the Vietnam War, reserve component soldiers have taken a more active role in U.S. military operations. For example, Reserve and Guard units took part in the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), peacekeeping in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), Afghanistan, and the [2003 invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq").

See [Structure of the United States Army](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Army "Structure of the United States Army") for a detailed treatment of the [history](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Army#History "Structure of the United States Army"), [components](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Army#Active_and_reserve_components "Structure of the United States Army"), [administrative and operational structure](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Army#Administrative "Structure of the United States Army") and the [branches and functional areas](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Army#Branches_and_functional_areas "Structure of the United States Army") of the Army.

The U.S. Army is made up of three components: the active component, the Regular Army; and two reserve components, the Army National Guard and the Army Reserve. Both reserve components are primarily composed of part-time soldiers who train once a month – known as [battle assemblies](/wiki/Battle_Assembly "Battle Assembly") or unit training assemblies (UTAs) – and conduct two to three weeks of annual training each year. Both the Regular Army and the Army Reserve are organized under [Title 10 of the United States Code](/wiki/Title_10_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 10 of the United States Code"), while the National Guard is organized under [Title 32](/wiki/Title_32_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 32 of the United States Code"). While the Army National Guard is organized, trained, and equipped as a component of the U.S. Army, when it is not in federal service it is under the command of individual state and territorial governors. However, the [District of Columbia National Guard](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_National_Guard "District of Columbia National Guard") reports to the U.S. president, not the [district's mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_District_of_Columbia "Mayor of the District of Columbia"), even when not federalized. Any or all of the [National Guard](/wiki/National_Guard_of_the_United_States "National Guard of the United States") can be federalized by presidential order and against the governor's wishes.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

The U.S. Army is led by a civilian [secretary of the Army](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Army "United States Secretary of the Army"), who has the statutory authority to conduct all the affairs of the army under the authority, direction, and control of the [secretary of defense](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Defense "United States Secretary of Defense").[[124]](#cite_note-126) The [chief of staff of the Army](/wiki/Chief_of_Staff_of_the_United_States_Army "Chief of Staff of the United States Army"), who is the highest-ranked military officer in the army, serves as the principal military adviser and executive agent for the secretary of the Army, i.e., its service chief; and as a member of the [Joint Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Joint Chiefs of Staff"), a body composed of the service chiefs from each of the four military services belonging to the Department of Defense who advise the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), the secretary of defense and the [National Security Council](/wiki/United_States_National_Security_Council "United States National Security Council") on operational military matters, under the guidance of the [chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff") and [vice chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff](/wiki/Vice_Chairman_of_the_Joint_Chiefs_of_Staff "Vice Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff").[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) In 1986, the [Goldwater–Nichols Act](/wiki/Goldwater%E2%80%93Nichols_Act "Goldwater–Nichols Act") mandated that operational control of the services follows a chain of command from the president to the secretary of defense directly to the [unified combatant commanders](/wiki/Unified_Combatant_Command "Unified Combatant Command"), who have control of all units in their geographic or functional area of responsibility, thus the secretaries of the military departments (and their respective service chiefs underneath them) only have the responsibility to organize, train and equip their service components. The army provides trained forces to the combatant commanders for use as directed by the secretary of defense.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

In 2013, the army shifted to six geographical commands that align with the six geographical unified combatant commands (CCMD):

* [United States Army Central](/wiki/Third_United_States_Army "Third United States Army") headquartered at [Shaw Air Force Base](/wiki/Shaw_Air_Force_Base "Shaw Air Force Base"), South Carolina[[97]](#cite_note-:2-99)
* [United States Army North](/wiki/Fifth_United_States_Army "Fifth United States Army") headquartered at [Fort Sam Houston](/wiki/Fort_Sam_Houston "Fort Sam Houston"), Texas[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [United States Army South](/wiki/United_States_Army_South "United States Army South") headquartered at Fort Sam Houston, Texas[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [United States Army Europe](/wiki/United_States_Army_Europe "United States Army Europe") and Africa headquartered at [Clay Kaserne](/wiki/Clay_Kaserne "Clay Kaserne"), Wiesbaden, Germany[[128]](#cite_note-130)
* [United States Army Pacific](/wiki/United_States_Army_Pacific_Command "United States Army Pacific Command") headquartered at [Fort Shafter](/wiki/Fort_Shafter "Fort Shafter"), Hawaii[[96]](#cite_note-:1-98)

The army also transformed its base unit from [divisions](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)") to [brigades](/wiki/Brigade_(United_States_Army) "Brigade (United States Army)"). Division lineage will be retained, but the divisional headquarters will be able to command any brigade, not just brigades that carry their divisional lineage. The central part of this plan is that each brigade will be modular, i.e., all brigades of the same type will be exactly the same and thus any brigade can be commanded by any division. As specified before the 2013 end-strength re-definitions, the three major types of brigade combat teams are:

* [Armored](/wiki/Brigade_Combat_Team#Heavy_brigade_combat_team "Brigade Combat Team") brigades, with a strength of 4,743 troops as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit).
* [Stryker](/wiki/Stryker_Brigade_Combat_Team "Stryker Brigade Combat Team") brigades, with a strength of 4,500 troops as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit).
* [Infantry](/wiki/Brigade_Combat_Team#Infantry_Brigade_Combat_Team "Brigade Combat Team") brigades, with a strength of 4,413 troops as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit).

In addition, there are combat support and service support modular brigades. Combat support brigades include [aviation](/wiki/Combat_Aviation_Brigade "Combat Aviation Brigade") (CAB) brigades, which will come in heavy and light varieties, [fires](/wiki/Fires_Brigade "Fires Brigade") (artillery) brigades (now transforms to division artillery) and [expeditionary military intelligence brigades](/wiki/Battlefield_Surveillance_Brigade "Battlefield Surveillance Brigade"). [Combat service support](/wiki/Combat_service_support "Combat service support") brigades include [sustainment](/wiki/Sustainment_Brigade "Sustainment Brigade") brigades and come in several varieties and serve the standard support role in an army.

### Combat maneuver organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Combat maneuver organizations")]

:   *To track the effects of the 2018 budget cuts, see [Transformation of the United States Army#Divisions and brigades](/wiki/Transformation_of_the_United_States_Army#Divisions_and_brigades "Transformation of the United States Army")*

The U.S. Army's conventional combat capability currently consists of 12 active divisions as well as several independent maneuver units. The 2nd Infantry Division, based in South Korea, is the only active duty division that does not have permanently assigned brigade combat teams (BCTs). The 2nd Infantry Division relies on rotational Stryker BCTs from other divisions based stateside to fulfill the necessary combat-arms role.

From 2013 through 2017, the Army sustained organizational and end-strength reductions after several years of [growth](/wiki/Grow_the_Army "Grow the Army"). In June 2013, the Army announced plans to downsize to 32 active brigade combat teams by 2015 to match a reduction in active-duty strength to 490,000 soldiers. Army Chief of Staff Raymond Odierno projected that the Army was to shrink to "450,000 in the active component, 335,000 in the National Guard and 195,000 in U.S. Army Reserve" by 2018.[[129]](#cite_note-131) However, this plan was scrapped by the incoming Trump administration, with subsequent plans to expand the Army by 16,000 soldiers to a total of 476,000 by October 2017. The National Guard and the Army Reserve will see a smaller expansion.[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133)

The Army's maneuver organization was most recently altered by the reorganization of [United States Army Alaska](/wiki/United_States_Army_Alaska "United States Army Alaska") into the [11th Airborne Division](/wiki/11th_Airborne_Division_(United_States) "11th Airborne Division (United States)"), transferring the 1st and 4th Brigade Combat Teams of the [25th Infantry Division](/wiki/25th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "25th Infantry Division (United States)") under a separate operational headquarters to reflect the brigades' distinct, Arctic-oriented mission. As part of the reorganization, the 1–11 (formerly 1–25) Stryker Brigade Combat Team will reorganize as an Infantry Brigade Combat Team.[[132]](#cite_note-134) Following this transition, the active component BCTs will number 11 Armored brigades, 6 Stryker brigades, and 14 Infantry brigades.

Within the Army National Guard and United States Army Reserve, there are a further eight divisions, 27 brigade combat teams, additional combat support and combat service support brigades, and independent cavalry, infantry, artillery, aviation, engineer, and support battalions. The Army Reserve in particular provides virtually all psychological operations and civil affairs units.

Additional capabilities include [special reconnaissance](/wiki/Special_reconnaissance "Special reconnaissance"), [air assault](/wiki/Air_assault "Air assault"), and direct action raids seizing key terrain such as airfields, destroying or securing strategic facilities, and capturing or killing enemies of the Nation. The Regiment also helps develop the equipment, technologies, training, and readiness that bridge the gap between special operations and traditional combat maneuver organizations.[[149]](#cite_note-151) |

Kennedy Special Warfare Center and School") | [Fort Bragg](/wiki/Fort_Bragg "Fort Bragg"), North Carolina | Selects and trains special forces, civil affairs, and psychological operations soldiers, consisting of two groups and other various training units and offices.[[150]](#cite_note-152) |

Under the control of [Joint Special Operations Command](/wiki/Joint_Special_Operations_Command "Joint Special Operations Command"), SFOD–D specializes in [hostage rescue](/wiki/Hostage "Hostage"), [counter-terrorism](/wiki/Counter-terrorism "Counter-terrorism"), [direct action](/wiki/Direct_action_(military) "Direct action (military)"), and [special reconnaissance](/wiki/Special_reconnaissance "Special reconnaissance") against [high-value targets](/wiki/High-value_target "High-value target") via eight squadrons: four assault, one aviation, one clandestine, one combat support, and one nuclear disposal.[[151]](#cite_note-153)[[152]](#cite_note-154) |

## Medical Department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Medical Department")]

Main article: [Army Medical Department (United States)](/wiki/Army_Medical_Department_(United_States) "Army Medical Department (United States)")

The [United States Army Medical Department](/wiki/Army_Medical_Department_(United_States) "Army Medical Department (United States)") (AMEDD), formerly the Army Medical Service (AMS), is the primary healthcare organization of the United States Army and is led by the [Surgeon General of the United States Army](/wiki/Surgeon_General_of_the_United_States_Army "Surgeon General of the United States Army") (TSG), a three-star [lieutenant general](/wiki/Lieutenant_general_(United_States) "Lieutenant general (United States)"), who (by policy) also serves as the Commanding General, [United States Army Medical Command](/wiki/United_States_Army_Medical_Command "United States Army Medical Command") (MEDCOM). TSG is assisted by a Deputy Surgeon General and a full staff, the Office of the Surgeon General (OTSG). The incumbent Surgeon General is Lieutenant General [Mary K. Izaguirre](/wiki/Mary_Krueger "Mary Krueger") (since 25 January 2024).[[153]](#cite_note-155)

AMEDD encompasses the Army's six non-combat, medical-focused specialty branches (or "Corps"), these branches are: the [Medical Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Medical_Corps "United States Army Medical Corps"), [Nurse Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Nurse_Corps "United States Army Nurse Corps"), [Dental Corps](/wiki/Army_Medical_Department_(United_States) "Army Medical Department (United States)"), [Veterinary Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Veterinary_Corps "United States Army Veterinary Corps"), [Medical Service Corps](/wiki/Army_Medical_Department_(United_States) "Army Medical Department (United States)"), and [Medical Specialist Corps](/wiki/Army_Medical_Department_(United_States) "Army Medical Department (United States)"). Each of these branches is headed by a Corps Chief that reports directly to the Surgeon General.[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-157)[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159)[[158]](#cite_note-160)

## Personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Personnel")]

See also: [List of ranks used by the United States Army](/wiki/List_of_ranks_used_by_the_United_States_Army "List of ranks used by the United States Army")

The Army's Talent Management Task Force (TMTF) has deployed IPPS-A,[[159]](#cite_note-ipps-a-161) the [Integrated Personnel and Pay System - Army](/wiki/Integrated_Personnel_and_Pay_System_-_Army "Integrated Personnel and Pay System - Army"), an app which serves the National Guard, and on 17 January 2023 the Army Reserve and Active Army.[[160]](#cite_note-golive-162) Soldiers were reminded to update their information using the legacy systems to keep their payroll and personnel information current by December 2021. IPPS-A is the Human Resources system for the Army, is available for download for Android, or the Apple store.[[161]](#cite_note-ipps-aApple-163) It will be used for future promotions and other personnel decisions. Among the changes are:

* BCAP, the Battalion Commander Assessment Program. In January 2020, over 800 majors and lieutenant colonels from all over the Army converged on Fort Knox to take part in a five-day program to select the next battalion commanders for the Army (beginning in FY2021). This process replaces the former selection process which was based solely on rank and individual reviews of past performance. From now on, more consideration will be given to an individual officer's personal preference, as part of 25 other selection criteria.[[162]](#cite_note-164) "Promotion boards will now be able to see almost all substantiated adverse information".[[163]](#cite_note-adverseInfo-165) The promotion boards will be able to see anything in an officer's human resource record. Officers are encouraged to become familiar with their human resource record, and to file rebuttals to adverse information.[[163]](#cite_note-adverseInfo-165)
* Depending on the success of this initiative, other assessment programs could be instituted as well, for promotion to sergeants major,[[164]](#cite_note-smap-166) and for assessment of colonels for command.[[165]](#cite_note-ccap-167)

Below are the U.S. Army ranks authorized for use today and their equivalent NATO designations. Although no living officer currently holds the rank of [General of the Army](/wiki/General_of_the_Army_(United_States) "General of the Army (United States)"), it is still authorized by Congress for use in wartime.

### Officers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Officers")]

Main article: [United States Army officer rank insignia](/wiki/United_States_Army_officer_rank_insignia "United States Army officer rank insignia")

There are several paths to becoming a [commissioned officer](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer")[[166]](#cite_note-futuresoldiers-168) including the [United States Military Academy](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy "United States Military Academy"),[[167]](#cite_note-169) [Reserve Officers' Training Corps](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps"),[[168]](#cite_note-170) [Officer Candidate School](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School "Officer Candidate School"),[[169]](#cite_note-171) and [direct commissioning](/wiki/Direct_commission_officer "Direct commission officer"). Regardless of which road an officer takes, the insignia are the same. Certain professions including physicians, pharmacists, nurses, lawyers and chaplains are commissioned directly into the Army.

Most army commissioned officers (those who are generalists)[[170]](#cite_note-reformers-172) are promoted based on an "up or out" system. A more flexible talent management process is underway.[[170]](#cite_note-reformers-172) The [Defense Officer Personnel Management Act](/wiki/Defense_Officer_Personnel_Management_Act "Defense Officer Personnel Management Act") of 1980 establishes rules for the timing of promotions and limits the number of officers that can serve at any given time.[[171]](#cite_note-173)

Army regulations call for addressing all personnel with the rank of general as "General (last name)" regardless of the number of stars. Likewise, both colonels and lieutenant colonels are addressed as "Colonel (last name)" and first and second lieutenants as "Lieutenant (last name)".[[172]](#cite_note-ar600-174)

### Warrant officers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Warrant officers")]

Main article: [Warrant officer (United States)](/wiki/Warrant_officer_(United_States) "Warrant officer (United States)")

[Warrant officers](/wiki/Warrant_officer_(United_States) "Warrant officer (United States)")[[166]](#cite_note-futuresoldiers-168) are single track, specialty officers with subject matter expertise in a particular area. They are initially appointed as warrant officers (in the rank of WO1) by the [secretary of the Army](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Army "United States Secretary of the Army"), but receive their [commission](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces)#Warrant_officers "Officer (armed forces)") upon promotion to chief warrant officer two (CW2).

By regulation, warrant officers are addressed as "Mr. (last name)" or "Ms. (last name)" by senior officers and as "sir" or "ma'am" by all enlisted personnel.[[172]](#cite_note-ar600-174) However, many personnel address warrant officers as "Chief (last name)" within their units regardless of rank.

### Enlisted personnel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Enlisted personnel")]

Main article: [United States Army enlisted rank insignia](/wiki/United_States_Army_enlisted_rank_insignia "United States Army enlisted rank insignia")

See also: [Enlisted rank](/wiki/Enlisted_rank "Enlisted rank")

Sergeants and corporals are referred to as NCOs, short for [non-commissioned officers](/wiki/Staff_Noncommissioned_Officer "Staff Noncommissioned Officer").[[166]](#cite_note-futuresoldiers-168)[[173]](#cite_note-symbolsandinsig-176) This distinguishes corporals from the more numerous specialists who have the same pay grade but do not exercise leadership responsibilities. Beginning in 2021, all corporals will be required to conduct [structured self-development](/wiki/Structured_self-development "Structured self-development") for the NCO ranks, completing the [basic leader course](/wiki/Basic_leader_course "Basic leader course") (BLC), or else be laterally assigned as specialists. Specialists who have completed BLC and who have been recommended for promotion will be permitted to wear corporal rank before their recommended promotion as NCOs.[[174]](#cite_note-lacdan-177)

Privates and privates first class (E3) are addressed as "Private (last name)", specialists as "Specialist (last name)", corporals as "Corporal (last name)" and sergeants, staff sergeants, sergeants first class and master sergeants all as "Sergeant (last name)". First sergeants are addressed as "First Sergeant (last name)" and sergeants major and command sergeants major are addressed as "Sergeant Major (last name)".[[172]](#cite_note-ar600-174)[[175]](#cite_note-178)
