### Training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Training")]

Main article: [United States Army Basic Training](/wiki/United_States_Army_Basic_Training "United States Army Basic Training")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Rangers_from_the_75th_Ranger_Regiment_fast-rope_from_an_MH-47_Chinook_during_a_capabilities_exercise.jpg/250px-Rangers_from_the_75th_Ranger_Regiment_fast-rope_from_an_MH-47_Chinook_during_a_capabilities_exercise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rangers_from_the_75th_Ranger_Regiment_fast-rope_from_an_MH-47_Chinook_during_a_capabilities_exercise.jpg)

U.S. Army Rangers practicing [fast roping](/wiki/Fast-roping "Fast-roping") techniques from an [MH-47](/wiki/Boeing_CH-47_Chinook "Boeing CH-47 Chinook") during an exercise at [Fort Bragg](/wiki/Fort_Bragg "Fort Bragg")

Training in the U.S. Army is generally divided into two categories – individual and collective. Because of COVID-19 precautions, the first two weeks of [basic training](/wiki/Basic_training "Basic training") — not including processing and out-processing – incorporate social distancing and indoor desk-oriented training. Once the recruits have tested negative for COVID-19 for two weeks, the remaining 8 weeks follow the traditional activities for most recruits,[[177]](#cite_note-183) followed by Advanced Individualized Training (AIT) where they receive training for their [military occupational specialties (MOS)](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Army_careers "List of United States Army careers").[[178]](#cite_note-184) Some individual's MOSs range anywhere from 14 to 20 weeks of One Station Unit Training (OSUT), which combines Basic Training and AIT. The length of AIT school varies by the MOS. The length of time spent in AIT depends on the soldier's MOS. Certain highly technical MOS training requires many months (e.g., foreign language translators). Depending on the army's needs, [Basic Combat Training](/wiki/United_States_Army_Basic_Training#Basic_Combat_Training "United States Army Basic Training") for combat arms soldiers is conducted at several locations. Still, two of the longest-running are the Armor School and the [Infantry School](/wiki/United_States_Army_Infantry_School "United States Army Infantry School"), both at [Fort Moore](/wiki/Fort_Moore "Fort Moore"), Georgia. Sergeant Major of the Army Dailey notes that an infantrymen's pilot program for [One Station Unit Training](/wiki/One_Station_Unit_Training "One Station Unit Training") (OSUT) extends 8 weeks beyond Basic Training and AIT, to 22 weeks. The pilot, designed to boost infantry readiness ended in December 2018. The new Infantry OSUT covered the [M240](/wiki/M240 "M240") machine gun as well as the [M249 squad automatic weapon](/wiki/M249_light_machine_gun "M249 light machine gun").[[179]](#cite_note-m240,249-185) The redesigned Infantry OSUT started in 2019.[[180]](#cite_note-daileyOSUT-186)[[181]](#cite_note-extendedOSUT-187) Depending on the result of the 2018 pilot, OSUTs could also extend training in other combat arms beyond the infantry.[[180]](#cite_note-daileyOSUT-186) One Station Unit Training will be extended to 22 weeks for Armor by Fiscal Year 2021.[[29]](#cite_note-Strategy2018-30) Additional OSUTs are expanding to Cavalry, Engineer, and Military Police (MP) in the succeeding Fiscal Years.[[182]](#cite_note-addedOsuts-188)

A new training assignment for junior officers was instituted, that they serve as platoon leaders for Basic Combat Training (BCT) platoons.[[183]](#cite_note-LTsAsBCT-189) These lieutenants will assume many of the administrative, logistical, and day-to-day tasks formerly performed by the drill sergeants of those platoons and are expected to "lead, train, and assist with maintaining and enhancing the morale, welfare and readiness" of the drill sergeants and their BCT platoons.[[183]](#cite_note-LTsAsBCT-189) These lieutenants are also expected to stem any inappropriate behaviors they witness in their platoons, to free up the drill sergeants for training.[[183]](#cite_note-LTsAsBCT-189)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/101st-Airborne-Soldiers-build-elite-Iraqi-force-with-Ranger-Training-7-480x319.jpg/250px-101st-Airborne-Soldiers-build-elite-Iraqi-force-with-Ranger-Training-7-480x319.jpg)](/wiki/File:101st-Airborne-Soldiers-build-elite-Iraqi-force-with-Ranger-Training-7-480x319.jpg)

A trainer with Company A, 1st Battalion 502nd Infantry Regiment, Task Force Strike, [101st Airborne Division](/wiki/101st_Airborne_Division "101st Airborne Division") assisting Iraqi army ranger students during a room clearing drill at Camp Taji, Iraq on 18 July 2016

The [United States Army Combat Fitness Test](/wiki/United_States_Army_Combat_Fitness_Test "United States Army Combat Fitness Test") (ACFT) was introduced in 2018 to 60 battalions spread throughout the Army.[[184]](#cite_note-6acftEvents-190) The test and scoring system is the same for all soldiers, regardless of gender. It takes an hour to complete, including rest periods.[[185]](#cite_note-speed-191) The ACFT supersedes the Army Physical Fitness Test (APFT),[[186]](#cite_note-apftInterimCovid-192)[[187]](#cite_note-apftUseForRetention-193)[[188]](#cite_note-194) as being more relevant to survival in combat.[[184]](#cite_note-6acftEvents-190) Six events were determined to better predict which muscle groups of the body were adequately conditioned for combat actions:[[185]](#cite_note-speed-191) three deadlifts,[[189]](#cite_note-195) a standing power throw of a ten-pound medicine ball,[[190]](#cite_note-196) hand-release pushups[[191]](#cite_note-197) (which replace the traditional pushup), a sprint/drag/carry 250 yard event,[[192]](#cite_note-198) three pull-ups with leg tucks (or a plank test in lieu of the leg tuck),[[193]](#cite_note-199)[[194]](#cite_note-200) a mandatory rest period, and a two-mile run.[[195]](#cite_note-videoACFT-201) As of 1 October 2020 all soldiers from all three components (Regular Army, Reserve, and National Guard)[[196]](#cite_note-202) are subject to this test.[[197]](#cite_note-allSoldiersOct2020-203)[[198]](#cite_note-COs,CSMsOnACFT-204) The ACFT now tests all soldiers in basic training as of October 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit). The ACFT became the official test of record on 1 October 2020; before that day, every Army unit was required to complete a diagnostic ACFT[[199]](#cite_note-acftKyGuard1st-205) (All Soldiers with valid APFT scores can use them until March 2022. The Holistic Health and Fitness (H2F) System is one way that soldiers can prepare.).[[200]](#cite_note-grinston2020acft-206)[[201]](#cite_note-prepForAcft-207)[[202]](#cite_note-h2fColocatesSharp-208) The ACFT movements directly translate to movements on the battlefield.[[181]](#cite_note-extendedOSUT-187) Following their basic and advanced training at the individual level, soldiers may choose to continue their training and apply for an "additional skill identifier" (ASI). The ASI allows the army to take a wide-ranging MOS and focus it on a more specific MOS. For example, a combat medic, whose duties are to provide pre-hospital emergency treatment, may receive ASI training to become a cardiovascular specialist, a dialysis specialist, or even a licensed practical nurse. For commissioned officers, training includes pre-commissioning training, known as Basic Officer Leader Course A, either at [USMA](/wiki/West_Point "West Point") or via [ROTC](/wiki/ROTC "ROTC"), or by completing [OCS](/wiki/Officer_Candidate_School_(U.S._Army) "Officer Candidate School (U.S. Army)"). After commissioning, officers undergo branch-specific training at the Basic Officer Leaders Course B, (formerly called Officer Basic Course), which varies in time and location according to their future assignments. Officers will continue to attend standardized training at different stages of their careers.[[203]](#cite_note-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Yudh_Abhyas_2015_Soldiers_familiarize_with_INSAS_1B1.jpg/330px-Yudh_Abhyas_2015_Soldiers_familiarize_with_INSAS_1B1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yudh_Abhyas_2015_Soldiers_familiarize_with_INSAS_1B1.jpg)

U.S. Army soldiers familiarizing with the latest INSAS 1B1 during exercise Yudh Abhyas 2015

Collective training at the unit level takes place at the unit's assigned station, but the most intensive training at higher echelons is conducted at the three combat training centers (CTC); the [National Training Center](/wiki/National_Training_Center "National Training Center") (NTC) at [Fort Irwin](/wiki/Fort_Irwin "Fort Irwin"), California, the [Joint Readiness Training Center](/wiki/Fort_Johnson#JRTC_moves_to_Polk "Fort Johnson") (JRTC) at [Fort Johnson](/wiki/Fort_Johnson "Fort Johnson"), Louisiana and the [Joint Multinational Training Center](/wiki/Grafenwoehr_Training_Area "Grafenwoehr Training Area") (JMRC) at the Hohenfels Training Area in [Hohenfels and Grafenwöhr](/wiki/Hohenfels,_Bavaria "Hohenfels, Bavaria"),[[204]](#cite_note-210) Germany. [ReARMM](/wiki/ARFORGEN "ARFORGEN") is the Army Force Generation process approved in 2020 to meet the need to continuously replenish forces for deployment at the unit level and for other echelons as required by the mission. Individual-level replenishment still requires training at a unit level, which is conducted at the continental U.S. (CONUS) replacement center (CRC) at [Fort Bliss](/wiki/Fort_Bliss "Fort Bliss"), in New Mexico and Texas before their individual deployment.[[205]](#cite_note-211)

#### Future Soldier Prep Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Future Soldier Prep Course")]

The United States Army has faced recruiting challenges since the COVID-19 pandemic. The Army has implemented the Future Soldier Prep Course (FSPC) to address these issues. This program is designed to assist potential recruits who may initially need to meet the Army's physical fitness or academic standards.[[206]](#cite_note-n424-212)

In the fiscal year ending 30 September 2023, approximately 13,000 of the 55,000 recruits, or 24%, participated in the FSPC. This indicates a significant reliance on the program to fill recruitment quotas.[[206]](#cite_note-n424-212)

The FSPC offers both physical fitness and academic training. However, most participants enroll in the academic component, which focuses on subjects like basic math, English, and other essential skills.[[206]](#cite_note-n424-212)

## Equipment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Equipment")]

Main article: [List of equipment of the United States Army](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_United_States_Army "List of equipment of the United States Army")

The [chief of staff of the Army](/wiki/Chief_of_Staff_of_the_United_States_Army "Chief of Staff of the United States Army") has identified six modernization priorities, these being (in order): artillery, ground vehicles, aircraft, network, air/missile defense, and soldier lethality.[[207]](#cite_note-asawsh2018-213)

### Weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Weapons")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/The_first_of_two_Terminal_High_Altitude_Area_Defense_%28THAAD%29_interceptors_is_launched_during_a_successful_intercept_test_-_US_Army.jpg/250px-The_first_of_two_Terminal_High_Altitude_Area_Defense_%28THAAD%29_interceptors_is_launched_during_a_successful_intercept_test_-_US_Army.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_first_of_two_Terminal_High_Altitude_Area_Defense_(THAAD)_interceptors_is_launched_during_a_successful_intercept_test_-_US_Army.jpg)

A [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin") [Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD)](/wiki/Terminal_High_Altitude_Area_Defense "Terminal High Altitude Area Defense") system used for ballistic missile protection

#### Individual weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Individual weapons")]

The United States Army employs various weapons to provide light firepower at short ranges. The most common weapon type used by the army is the [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine"), a compact variant of the [M16 rifle](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle"),[[208]](#cite_note-214) which is being replaced gradually by the [M7 rifle](/wiki/XM7_rifle "XM7 rifle") among close combat units.[[209]](#cite_note-215) The primary sidearm in the U.S. Army is the [M17](/wiki/SIG_Sauer_M17 "SIG Sauer M17") pistol[[210]](#cite_note-216) through the [Modular Handgun System](/wiki/Modular_Handgun_System "Modular Handgun System") program.[[211]](#cite_note-217) Soldiers are also equipped with various [hand grenades](/wiki/Hand_grenade "Hand grenade"), such as the [M67 fragmentation grenade](/wiki/M67_grenade "M67 grenade") and [M18 smoke grenade](/wiki/Smoke_grenade "Smoke grenade").[[212]](#cite_note-218)[[213]](#cite_note-219)

Many units are supplemented with a variety of specialized weapons, including the [M249 SAW](/wiki/M249_light_machine_gun "M249 light machine gun") (Squad Automatic Weapon), to provide suppressive fire at the squad level.[[214]](#cite_note-220) Some units, such as the [10th Mountain Division](/wiki/10th_Mountain_Division "10th Mountain Division"), have replaced their M249s with [Mk 48s](/wiki/Mk_48 "Mk 48").[[215]](#cite_note-221) Indirect fire is provided by the [M320 grenade launcher](/wiki/M320_Grenade_Launcher_Module "M320 Grenade Launcher Module").[[216]](#cite_note-222) The [M1014 Joint Service Combat Shotgun](/wiki/Benelli_M4_Super_90 "Benelli M4 Super 90") or the [Mossberg 590 Shotgun](/wiki/Mossberg_500#Model_500_vs._Model_590_vs._Model_590A1 "Mossberg 500") are used for [door breaching](/wiki/Door_breaching "Door breaching") and close-quarters combat. The [M14EBR](/wiki/Mk_14_Enhanced_Battle_Rifle "Mk 14 Enhanced Battle Rifle") is used by designated marksmen. Snipers use the [M107 Long Range Sniper Rifle](/wiki/M82_Barrett_rifle "M82 Barrett rifle"), the [M2010 Enhanced Sniper Rifle](/wiki/M2010_Enhanced_Sniper_Rifle "M2010 Enhanced Sniper Rifle") and the [M110 Semi-Automatic Sniper Rifle](/wiki/M110_Semi-Automatic_Sniper_System "M110 Semi-Automatic Sniper System").[[217]](#cite_note-223)

#### Crew-served weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Crew-served weapons")]

The army employs various crew-served weapons to provide heavy firepower at ranges exceeding that of individual weapons.

The [M240](/wiki/M240_machine_gun "M240 machine gun") is the U.S. Army's standard Medium Machine Gun.[[218]](#cite_note-224) The [M2 heavy machine gun](/wiki/M2_Browning_machine_gun "M2 Browning machine gun") is generally used as a vehicle-mounted machine gun. In the same way, the 40 mm [MK 19 grenade machine gun](/wiki/Mk_19_grenade_launcher "Mk 19 grenade launcher") is mainly used by motorized units.[[219]](#cite_note-225)

The U.S. Army uses three types of [mortar](/wiki/Mortar_(weapon) "Mortar (weapon)") for indirect fire support when heavier artillery may not be appropriate or available. The smallest of these is the 60 mm [M224](/wiki/M224_mortar "M224 mortar"), normally assigned at the infantry company level.[[220]](#cite_note-226) At the next higher echelon, infantry battalions are typically supported by a section of 81 mm [M252 mortars](/wiki/M252_mortar "M252 mortar").[[221]](#cite_note-227) The largest mortar in the army's inventory is the 120 mm [M120/M121](/wiki/M120_mortar "M120 mortar"), which is usually employed by mechanized units.[[222]](#cite_note-228)

Fire support for light infantry units is provided by towed howitzers, including the 105 mm [M119A1](/wiki/M119_howitzer "M119 howitzer")[[223]](#cite_note-229) and the 155 mm [M777](/wiki/M777_howitzer "M777 howitzer").[[224]](#cite_note-230)

The U.S. Army utilizes a variety of direct-fire rockets and missiles to provide infantry with an Anti-Armor Capability. The [AT4](/wiki/AT4 "AT4") is an unguided projectile that can destroy armor and bunkers at ranges up to 500 meters.[[225]](#cite_note-231) The [FIM-92 Stinger](/wiki/FIM-92_Stinger "FIM-92 Stinger") is a shoulder-launched, heat seeking anti-aircraft missile.[[226]](#cite_note-232) The [FGM-148 Javelin](/wiki/FGM-148_Javelin "FGM-148 Javelin") and [BGM-71 TOW](/wiki/BGM-71_TOW "BGM-71 TOW") are anti-tank guided missiles.[[227]](#cite_note-233)[[228]](#cite_note-234)

### Vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Vehicles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Patrol_in_Iraq%2C_March_2008.jpg/250px-Patrol_in_Iraq%2C_March_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrol_in_Iraq,_March_2008.jpg)

A U.S. soldier on patrol in Iraq with the support of a [Humvee](/wiki/Humvee "Humvee") vehicle

U.S. Army doctrine puts a premium on mechanized warfare. It fields the highest vehicle-to-soldier ratio in the world as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit).[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18)[[229]](#cite_note-235) The army's most common vehicle is the High Mobility Multipurpose Wheeled Vehicle (HMMWV), commonly called the [Humvee](/wiki/Humvee "Humvee"), which is capable of serving as a cargo/troop carrier, weapons platform and ambulance, among many other roles.[[230]](#cite_note-236) While they operate a wide variety of combat support vehicles, one of the most common types centers on the family of [HEMTT](/wiki/HEMTT "HEMTT") vehicles. The [M1A2 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") is the army's [main battle tank](/wiki/Main_battle_tank "Main battle tank"),[[231]](#cite_note-237) while the [M2A3 Bradley](/wiki/M2_Bradley "M2 Bradley") is the standard [infantry fighting vehicle](/wiki/Infantry_fighting_vehicle "Infantry fighting vehicle").[[232]](#cite_note-238) Other vehicles include the [Stryker](/wiki/Stryker "Stryker"),[[233]](#cite_note-239) the [M113 armored personnel carrier](/wiki/M113_armored_personnel_carrier "M113 armored personnel carrier")[[234]](#cite_note-240) and multiple types of [Mine Resistant Ambush Protected](/wiki/MRAP "MRAP") (MRAP) vehicles.[[235]](#cite_note-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/3rd_ID_M1A1_Abrams_TC_and_Gunner_2008.jpg/250px-3rd_ID_M1A1_Abrams_TC_and_Gunner_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:3rd_ID_M1A1_Abrams_TC_and_Gunner_2008.jpg)

[3rd Infantry Division](/wiki/3rd_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "3rd Infantry Division (United States)") soldiers manning an [M1A1 Abrams](/wiki/M1_Abrams "M1 Abrams") in Iraq

The U.S. Army's principal [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") weapons are the [M109A7 Paladin](/wiki/M109_Paladin "M109 Paladin") self-propelled howitzer[[236]](#cite_note-242) and the [M270 multiple launch rocket system](/wiki/M270_multiple_launch_rocket_system "M270 multiple launch rocket system") (MLRS),[[237]](#cite_note-243) both mounted on tracked platforms and assigned to heavy mechanized units.

### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Aviation")]

While the [United States Army Aviation Branch](/wiki/United_States_Army_Aviation_Branch "United States Army Aviation Branch") operates a few [fixed-wing aircraft](/wiki/Fixed-wing_aircraft "Fixed-wing aircraft"), it mainly operates several types of rotary-wing aircraft. These include the [AH-64 Apache](/wiki/AH-64_Apache "AH-64 Apache") [attack helicopter](/wiki/Attack_helicopter "Attack helicopter"),[[238]](#cite_note-244) the [UH-60 Black Hawk](/wiki/UH-60_Black_Hawk "UH-60 Black Hawk") utility tactical transport helicopter[[239]](#cite_note-245) and the [CH-47 Chinook](/wiki/CH-47_Chinook "CH-47 Chinook") heavy-lift transport helicopter.[[240]](#cite_note-246) Restructuring plans call for reduction of 750 aircraft and from seven to four types.[[241]](#cite_note-247) The Army is evaluating two fixed-wing aircraft demonstrators; ARES, and Artemis are under evaluation to replace the Guardrail ISR (Intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance) aircraft.[[242]](#cite_note-248) Under the [Johnson-McConnell agreement of 1966](/wiki/Johnson-McConnell_agreement_of_1966 "Johnson-McConnell agreement of 1966"), the Army agreed to limit its fixed-wing aviation role to administrative mission support (light unarmed aircraft which cannot operate from forward positions). For [UAVs](/wiki/UAV "UAV"), the Army is deploying at least one company of drone [MQ-1C Gray Eagles](/wiki/MQ-1C_Gray_Eagle "MQ-1C Gray Eagle") to each Active Army division.[[243]](#cite_note-249)

### Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

Main article: [Uniforms of the United States Army](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_United_States_Army "Uniforms of the United States Army")

The [Army Combat Uniform](/wiki/Army_Combat_Uniform "Army Combat Uniform") (ACU) currently features a camouflage pattern known as [Operational Camouflage Pattern](/wiki/Operational_Camouflage_Pattern "Operational Camouflage Pattern") (OCP); OCP replaced a pixel-based pattern known as [Universal Camouflage Pattern](/wiki/Universal_Camouflage_Pattern "Universal Camouflage Pattern") (UCP) in 2019.

On 11 November 2018, the Army announced a new version of 'Army Greens' based on uniforms worn during World War II that will become the standard garrison service uniform.[[244]](#cite_note-ArmyGreens-250) The blue [Army Service Uniform](/wiki/Army_Service_Uniform "Army Service Uniform") will remain as the dress uniform. The Army Greens are projected to be first fielded in the summer of 2020.[[244]](#cite_note-ArmyGreens-250)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

* [![The 2020 Army Greens uniform](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/2020_Army_Greens_Uniform.png/250px-2020_Army_Greens_Uniform.png)](/wiki/File:2020_Army_Greens_Uniform.png "The 2020 Army Greens uniform")

  The 2020 Army Greens uniform

#### Berets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Berets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Wayne_Downing_funeral_honor_guard.jpg/250px-Wayne_Downing_funeral_honor_guard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wayne_Downing_funeral_honor_guard.jpg)

The Ranger Honor Platoon marching in their tan berets and former service uniform

The [beret flash](/wiki/United_States_military_beret_flash "United States military beret flash") of enlisted personnel displays their [distinctive unit insignia](/wiki/Distinctive_unit_insignia "Distinctive unit insignia") (shown above). The U.S. Army's black beret is no longer worn with the ACU for garrison duty, having been permanently replaced with the patrol cap. After years of complaints that it was not suited well for most work conditions, Army Chief of Staff General [Martin Dempsey](/wiki/Martin_Dempsey "Martin Dempsey") eliminated it for wear with the ACU in June 2011. Soldiers who are currently in a unit in jump status still wear berets, whether the wearer is parachute-qualified or not (maroon beret), while members of [Security Force Assistance Brigades](/wiki/Security_Force_Assistance_Brigade "Security Force Assistance Brigade") (SFABs) wear brown berets. Members of the 75th Ranger Regiment and the Airborne and Ranger Training Brigade (tan beret) and Special Forces (rifle green beret) may wear it with the Army Service Uniform for non-ceremonial functions. Unit commanders may still direct the wear of patrol caps in these units in training environments or motor pools.

### Tents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Army&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Tents")]

The Army has relied heavily on [tents](/wiki/Tent "Tent") to provide the various facilities needed while on deployment (Force Provider Expeditionary (FPE)).[[207]](#cite_note-asawsh2018-213): p.146 The most common tent uses for the military are as temporary [barracks](/wiki/Barracks "Barracks") (sleeping quarters), [DFAC](/wiki/Mess#U.S._Army "Mess") buildings (dining facilities),[[245]](#cite_note-251) forward operating bases (FOBs), after-action review (AAR), tactical operations center (TOC), morale, welfare and recreation (MWR) facilities, as well as security checkpoints. Furthermore, most of these tents are set up and operated through the support of [Natick Soldier Systems Center](/wiki/U.S._Army_Soldier_Systems_Center "U.S. Army Soldier Systems Center"). Each FPE contains billeting, latrines, showers, laundry and kitchen facilities for 50–150 Soldiers,[[207]](#cite_note-asawsh2018-213): p.146 and is stored in [Army Prepositioned Stocks 1, 2, 4 and 5](/wiki/Reorganization_plan_of_United_States_Army#Prepositioned_stocks "Reorganization plan of United States Army"). This provisioning allows combatant commanders to position soldiers as required in their [Area of Responsibility](/wiki/Area_of_Responsibility "Area of Responsibility"), within 24 to 48 hours.

The U.S. Army is beginning to use a more modern tent called the [deployable rapid assembly shelter](/wiki/Deployable_rapid_assembly_shelter "Deployable rapid assembly shelter") (DRASH). In 2008, DRASH became part of the Army's Standard Integrated Command Post System.[[246]](#cite_note-SICPS-252)
